title: ArrayList & Data Structures
description: Master ArrayList, HashMap, and Java Collections to store, search, sort, and manage groups of data.
learning_outcomes: Create and use ArrayList to store dynamic lists of objects
Add, remove, update, and search elements in an ArrayList
Loop through collections using for-each and iterators
Use HashMap to store key-value pairs
Sort collections using Comparable and Comparator
Understand the difference between arrays and ArrayList
Use wrapper classes (Integer, Double) with collections
Apply generics to create type-safe collections
Use common Collection methods: size, contains, indexOf, isEmpty
Build real-world applications using ArrayList and HashMap together

ai_tutor_config.personality: Encouraging, patient, uses real-world analogies. Never judges. Celebrates small wins. Explains collections like real-life containers -- shopping lists, phone books, and storage boxes.

intervention_triggers.idle_40_seconds: Hey, still with me? Collections can feel like a big jump from arrays. Think of ArrayList as a magic backpack that grows when you add items. Want me to walk through a quick example?
intervention_triggers.idle_90_seconds: Taking a moment to think is totally fine! If you are stuck on ArrayList vs array, here is the simple version: array = fixed box, ArrayList = stretchy bag. Same idea, more power. Want me to show you side by side?
intervention_triggers.idle_180_seconds: No rush at all! Collections have a lot of methods to remember. Take a short break if you need -- when you come back I will recap exactly where you left off and we can tackle it together step by step.
intervention_triggers.first_error: Nice try! Collection errors are very common -- even experienced developers forget things like importing java.util.ArrayList or using .get(i) instead of [i]. Let me show you exactly what happened.
intervention_triggers.repeated_errors: I can see this one is tricky. Let me try a different angle. Think of ArrayList like a numbered shopping list: you can add items at the end, insert in the middle, cross off any item, and the list renumbers itself. Want me to trace through your code line by line?
intervention_triggers.after_success: Brilliant! You just used one of the most important tools in Java. Almost every real application -- from social media feeds to banking systems -- uses ArrayList and HashMap. You are building real skills!

encouragement_phrases: Collections are the backbone of real Java applications -- you are learning essential skills!
Every time you use .add() or .get(), you are doing what professional developers do daily.
ArrayList is like upgrading from a bicycle to a car -- same journey, way more power!
HashMap is one of the most useful things in all of programming. Great job learning it!
Sorting and searching -- these are the skills that make your programs actually useful.
You are moving from beginner to intermediate Java developer right now. Feel proud!
That was a tough one and you solved it! Take a moment to appreciate that.
Mistakes with collections are totally normal -- even senior devs check the docs.
You are further along than you think. Look at all the exercises you have completed!
The fact that you are working through these shows real dedication.

explanation_styles.analogy: ArrayList is like a shopping list on your phone. You can add items, remove them, rearrange them, and the list grows or shrinks. Arrays are like a paper list -- fixed size, no flexibility.
explanation_styles.visual: Picture a row of numbered boxes: [0]=Apple [1]=Banana [2]=Cherry. You can insert Grape at position 1 and everything shifts: [0]=Apple [1]=Grape [2]=Banana [3]=Cherry.
explanation_styles.step_by_step: Step 1: import java.util.ArrayList. Step 2: ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(); Step 3: list.add("item"); Step 4: list.get(0) to read. Step 5: list.remove(0) to delete.
explanation_styles.code_trace: list.add("A"); list.add("B"); list.add(1,"X"); -- After add: [A,B]. After insert at 1: [A,X,B]. X pushed B to index 2.
explanation_styles.comparison: Array: fixed size, int[], uses []. ArrayList: grows/shrinks, ArrayList<Integer>, uses .get()/.add(). HashMap: key-value pairs like a dictionary.

theory.introduction:
So far, you have used arrays to store groups of data. Arrays are great, but they have a big limitation: once you create one, you cannot change its size. Imagine moving into a flat with exactly 5 shelves. What happens when you buy a 6th book? You need a whole new set of shelves!

Java Collections solve this problem. They are like SMART containers that grow and shrink automatically. The two most important ones are:

ArrayList -- like a flexible shopping list. Add items, remove items, search, sort, and the list handles the numbering.

HashMap -- like a phone book. Every entry has a KEY (name) and a VALUE (phone number). Look up any value instantly by its key.

Collections are used in virtually every real Java application: social media feeds (ArrayList of posts), user databases (HashMap of username to profile), shopping carts (ArrayList of products), leaderboards (sorted lists of scores). Learning collections means you can build real, useful software.
theory.why_important: Arrays have fixed sizes -- you must know the count in advance. In real applications, data changes constantly: users sign up, products get added, orders are placed. Collections handle this dynamically. They also provide built-in methods for searching, sorting, and filtering that would take dozens of lines with arrays. Professional Java code uses collections far more than raw arrays.

key_concepts.explanation:
ArrayList is like a magic backpack: it grows when you add items and shrinks when you remove them. Unlike arrays, you do not need to know the size in advance.

Key differences from array:
- Array: int[] nums = new int[5]; -- fixed at 5
- ArrayList: ArrayList<Integer> nums = new ArrayList<>(); -- starts empty, grows as needed

ArrayList uses GENERICS: the <Type> tells Java what it holds. ArrayList<String> holds only strings. This prevents mixing types accidentally.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of ArrayList<String> as a labelled box: this box ONLY holds strings. The <> is the label.

key_concepts.explanation:
ArrayList gives you multiple ways to add and remove:

add(item) -- adds to the END
add(index, item) -- INSERTS at position, shifts others right
remove(index) -- removes by position
remove(object) -- removes first matching object
set(index, item) -- REPLACES element at position
clear() -- removes everything

When you remove, everything after shifts LEFT to fill the gap. Like removing a card from a deck -- the rest slide over.
key_concepts.example:
ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>();
list.add("A");       // [A]
list.add("B");       // [A, B]
list.add("C");       // [A, B, C]
list.add(1, "X");    // [A, X, B, C]  -- inserted at 1
list.set(0, "Z");    // [Z, X, B, C]  -- replaced index 0
list.remove(2);      // [Z, X, C]     -- removed index 2 (B)
list.remove("X");    // [Z, C]        -- removed first 'X'
key_concepts.ai_hint: After remove, indexes shift! If you remove index 1 from [A,B,C], B is gone and C moves to index 1.

key_concepts.explanation:
ArrayList has built-in search methods -- no need to write loops:

contains(item) -- returns true/false
indexOf(item) -- returns position (-1 if not found)
lastIndexOf(item) -- last position of duplicates
isEmpty() -- true if list has zero elements
size() -- number of elements
key_concepts.example:
ArrayList<String> colours = new ArrayList<>();
colours.add("Red"); colours.add("Blue"); colours.add("Red");

System.out.println(colours.contains("Blue"));    // true
System.out.println(colours.contains("Green"));   // false
System.out.println(colours.indexOf("Red"));      // 0
System.out.println(colours.lastIndexOf("Red"));  // 2
System.out.println(colours.isEmpty());           // false
System.out.println(colours.size());              // 3
key_concepts.ai_hint: indexOf returns the FIRST match. lastIndexOf returns the LAST. Both return -1 if not found.

key_concepts.explanation:
Three ways to loop through ArrayList:

1. for-each loop (simplest, most common)
2. Traditional for loop with index (when you need position)
3. Iterator (when you need to remove during loop)

for-each is preferred 90% of the time. Use traditional for when you need the index number. Use Iterator when removing items while looping.
key_concepts.ai_hint: NEVER use list.remove() inside a for-each loop -- it crashes with ConcurrentModificationException. Use Iterator instead.

key_concepts.concept: Wrapper Classes -- Why <int> Does Not Work
key_concepts.explanation:
ArrayList only stores OBJECTS, not primitives. int, double, boolean are primitives.

Java provides wrapper classes: Integer for int, Double for double, Boolean for boolean.

Autoboxing: Java automatically converts between int and Integer. You write list.add(5) and Java converts 5 to Integer.valueOf(5) behind the scenes.

Unboxing: int x = list.get(0) converts Integer back to int automatically.
key_concepts.ai_hint: int -> Integer, double -> Double, char -> Character, boolean -> Boolean. Java converts automatically.

key_concepts.explanation:
Collections.sort() sorts an ArrayList in ascending order. For custom sorting, use Comparator.

Strings sort alphabetically. Numbers sort numerically. For custom objects, implement Comparable or pass a Comparator.

Collections.reverse() reverses the list. Collections.shuffle() randomises order.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Collections is a UTILITY CLASS with static methods. Do not confuse it with Collection (the interface).

key_concepts.explanation:
HashMap stores pairs: each KEY maps to a VALUE. Like a real dictionary: word (key) maps to definition (value).

Keys must be UNIQUE. Values can repeat. Lookup by key is extremely fast (constant time).

Common uses: username -> password, product code -> price, country -> capital.
key_concepts.ai_hint: put() with an existing key REPLACES the value. get() with a missing key returns null.

key_concepts.explanation:
Three ways to loop through a HashMap:

1. Loop through keys: keySet()
2. Loop through values: values()
3. Loop through both: entrySet()

entrySet() is most useful because you get both key AND value in each iteration.
key_concepts.ai_hint: HashMap does NOT guarantee order. If you need insertion order, use LinkedHashMap instead.

key_concepts.explanation:
ArrayList can hold ANY object type -- not just String and Integer. This is where collections meet OOP.

Store custom objects, loop through them, sort by any field. This is how real applications work: list of users, list of products, list of orders.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Override toString() in your class so printing ArrayList items looks good.

key_concepts.explanation:
Generics (<Type>) prevent you from mixing types in a collection.

Without generics (raw type): ArrayList list = new ArrayList(); -- can add ANYTHING, errors at runtime.
With generics: ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(); -- only String allowed, errors at COMPILE time.

Compile-time errors are MUCH better than runtime errors. Generics catch mistakes before your code runs.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Always use generics. ArrayList<String> not ArrayList. The diamond <> is your safety net.

key_concepts.explanation:
Top mistakes with collections:

1. Forgetting import java.util.ArrayList
2. Using array syntax [] instead of .get() and .add()
3. Removing during for-each loop (use Iterator)
4. Confusing remove(int index) with remove(Object) for Integer lists
5. Not checking null from HashMap.get()
6. Forgetting that HashMap keys must be unique

Best practices: always use generics, prefer for-each, check isEmpty before access, use getOrDefault for HashMap.
key_concepts.ai_hint: For Integer ArrayList: remove(1) removes by INDEX. remove(Integer.valueOf(1)) removes by VALUE.

key_concepts.concept: Array vs ArrayList vs HashMap -- When to Use Which
key_concepts.explanation:
Use ARRAY when: size is fixed and known, need primitives, maximum performance.

Use ARRAYLIST when: size changes, need add/remove, need search/sort methods.

Use HASHMAP when: need to look up values by a key, need fast key-based access, storing relationships.

In practice, ArrayList is used most often. HashMap is second. Raw arrays are rare in modern Java.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Ask: Do I know the size? -> Array. Will it change? -> ArrayList. Do I need key lookup? -> HashMap.

complete_examples.title: Example 1: Your First ArrayList
complete_examples.description: Create, add, access, and print an ArrayList of strings.
complete_examples.output:
All: [Apple, Banana, Cherry, Date]
Size: 4
First: Apple
Last: Date
Has Banana: true
complete_examples.ai_explanation: ArrayList starts empty. add() puts items at the end. get(0) reads the first item. size() counts items. contains() searches. Unlike arrays, no fixed size needed!
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add 'Elderberry' and print the updated list and new size.

complete_examples.title: Example 2: Adding, Inserting, and Removing
complete_examples.description: All the ways to modify an ArrayList.
complete_examples.output:
After adds: [A, B, C]
After insert: [A, X, B, C]
After set: [Z, X, B, C]
After remove index 2: [Z, X, C]
After remove Z: [X, C]
Size: 2
complete_examples.ai_explanation: add(item) appends. add(index,item) inserts and shifts. set(index,item) replaces. remove(index) and remove(object) both shrink the list. Everything after a removed item shifts left.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Start with [1,2,3,4,5]. Remove index 0 twice. What remains?

complete_examples.title: Example 3: ArrayList of Integers
complete_examples.description: Using wrapper class Integer with ArrayList and autoboxing.
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Integer not int in generics. Autoboxing converts: nums.add(50) becomes nums.add(Integer.valueOf(50)). Unboxing: for(int n : list) converts Integer back to int.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Calculate the average of the list.

complete_examples.title: Example 4: Searching an ArrayList
complete_examples.description: Find elements using contains, indexOf, and manual search.
complete_examples.output:
Contains Paris: true
Contains Rome: false
First London: 0
Last London: 3
Berlin at: 4
Rome at: -1
Long name: London at 0
Long name: London at 3
Long name: Berlin at 4
complete_examples.ai_explanation: indexOf returns -1 when not found. contains is true/false. For complex searches (like length > 5), use a loop. London appears twice: indexOf finds first (0), lastIndexOf finds last (3).
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a search that finds all cities starting with a specific letter.

complete_examples.title: Example 5: ArrayList of Custom Objects
complete_examples.description: Store, search, and display objects in an ArrayList.
complete_examples.output:
=== ALL PRODUCTS ===
Laptop ($999.99) x5
Mouse ($29.99) x50
Keyboard ($79.99) x30
Monitor ($349.99) x12

=== EXPENSIVE (>$100) ===
Laptop ($999.99) x5
Monitor ($349.99) x12

=== LOW STOCK (<20) ===
Laptop ($999.99) x5
Monitor ($349.99) x12
complete_examples.ai_explanation: ArrayList<Product> holds Product objects. for-each loop with conditions acts as a filter. toString() makes printing easy.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a method that returns the total value of all stock (price * stock for each product).

complete_examples.title: Example 6: HashMap Basics
complete_examples.description: Create, add, access, and loop through a HashMap.
complete_examples.output:
All: {Alice=95, Bob=82, Charlie=91, Diana=88}
Alice: 95
Has Bob: true
Has 100: false
Size: 4
Alice updated: 98
After remove: {Alice=98, Bob=82, Diana=88}

=== LOOP ===
Alice -> 98
Bob -> 82
Diana -> 88
complete_examples.ai_explanation: put(key,value) adds or updates. get(key) retrieves. containsKey and containsValue search. put with existing key REPLACES old value. Order is not guaranteed in HashMap.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add getOrDefault for a missing key. What does scores.getOrDefault("Eve", 0) return?

complete_examples.title: Example 7: HashMap Word Counter
complete_examples.description: Count word frequency using HashMap -- a classic interview question.
complete_examples.output:
Word counts: {mat=1, the=3, sat=1, cat=2, on=1}
Most common: the (3 times)
complete_examples.ai_explanation: getOrDefault(key, 0) returns 0 if key is missing. So freq.put(w, freq.getOrDefault(w, 0) + 1) either starts at 1 or increments. This pattern is used everywhere in data processing.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Modify to count character frequency instead of words.

complete_examples.title: Example 8: Sorting Custom Objects
complete_examples.description: Sort ArrayList of objects using Comparator.
complete_examples.output:
Original: [Charlie(78), Alice(92), Bob(85), Diana(92)]
By grade ASC: [Charlie(78), Bob(85), Alice(92), Diana(92)]
By grade DESC: [Alice(92), Diana(92), Bob(85), Charlie(78)]
By name: [Alice(92), Bob(85), Charlie(78), Diana(92)]
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Comparator tells sort HOW to compare. comparingInt extracts a number field. Lambda (a,b) -> b.grade-a.grade sorts descending. You can sort by ANY field.

complete_examples.title: Example 9: Iterator -- Safe Removal
complete_examples.description: Remove items while looping without ConcurrentModificationException.
complete_examples.ai_explanation: for-each + remove = crash. Iterator.remove() is the only safe way to remove during iteration. it.hasNext() checks if more exist. it.next() gets next item. it.remove() removes the last returned item.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Modify to remove numbers greater than 7 instead.

complete_examples.title: Example 10: ArrayList + HashMap Together
complete_examples.description: Real-world pattern: store lists inside a HashMap.
complete_examples.output:
Maths: [Alice, Bob] (2 students)
English: [Charlie, Alice] (2 students)
Science: [Bob, Diana, Alice] (3 students)
complete_examples.ai_explanation: HashMap<String, ArrayList<String>> maps class name to list of student names. Each value is an entire ArrayList. This pattern is used for grouping: categories with products, users with messages, etc.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Count which student appears in the most classes.

complete_examples.title: Example 11: Stack-like Operations
complete_examples.description: Use ArrayList as a stack: push, pop, peek.
complete_examples.output:
Stack: [Page 1, Page 2, Page 3]
Top: Page 3
Popped: Page 3
Stack: [Page 1, Page 2]
Popped: Page 2
Stack: [Page 1]
Empty: false
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Stack = Last In, First Out (LIFO). Like a stack of plates: the last one added is first removed. add() pushes to top. remove(size()-1) pops from top. This is how browser back buttons work!
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Implement an undo system: push actions, pop to undo.

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Filtering and Transforming
complete_examples.description: Create new lists by filtering and transforming an existing list.
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Filter: loop + condition + add to new list. Transform: loop + modify + add to new list. These patterns appear constantly in real programming -- processing data from databases, APIs, files.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Create a filter that keeps only numbers divisible by 3.

complete_examples.title: Example 13: Contact Book with HashMap
complete_examples.description: Build a functional contact book application.
complete_examples.output:
Added: Alice -> 07700-100
Added: Bob -> 07700-200
Added: Alex -> 07700-300
Added: Charlie -> 07700-400
=== CONTACTS (4) ===
  Alice: 07700-100
  Bob: 07700-200
  Alex: 07700-300
  Charlie: 07700-400
Search 'Bob': 07700-200
Search 'Eve': NOT FOUND
Names starting with 'Al':
  Alice: 07700-100
  Alex: 07700-300
complete_examples.ai_explanation: getOrDefault prevents null. keySet() lets you search through names. This is how phone apps and CRM systems work internally.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a deleteContact method and a countContacts method.

complete_examples.title: Example 14: Student Gradebook -- Full Application
complete_examples.description: Combines ArrayList, HashMap, sorting, and custom objects.
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Each Student has an ArrayList of grades. The roster is an ArrayList of Students. Sorting uses a Comparator by average (descending). This shows the real power of combining collections and objects.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a method to find the highest single grade across all students.

visual_diagram.title: Collections Decision Map -- ArrayList, HashMap & Choosing the Right Structure
visual_diagram.content: COLLECTIONS DECISION MAP [START] You need to store multiple items | v Do you know the exact size in advance? / \ YES NO | | v v Use ARRAY Do you need key-value lookup? int[] nums = new int[10]; / \ YES NO | | v v Use HASHMAP Use ARRAYLIST [ARRAYLIST OPERATIONS] CREATE: ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(); ADD: list.add("item") -- adds to END INSERT: list.add(1, "item") -- inserts at index GET: list.get(0) -- reads by index SET: list.set(0, "new") -- replaces at index REMOVE: list.remove(0) -- removes by index REMOVE: list.remove("item") -- removes by value SEARCH: list.contains("item") -- true/false FIND: list.indexOf("item") -- index or -1 SIZE: list.size() -- count SORT: Collections.sort(list) -- ascending [HASHMAP OPERATIONS] CREATE: HashMap<String,Integer> map = new HashMap<>(); PUT: map.put("key", value) -- adds or updates GET: map.get("key") -- value or null SAFE GET: map.getOrDefault("key",0) -- value or default REMOVE: map.remove("key") -- deletes pair SEARCH: map.containsKey("key") -- true/false SEARCH: map.containsValue(42) -- true/false LOOP: for(var e : map.entrySet()) -- key + value [WRAPPER CLASSES] int --> Integer (autoboxing) double --> Double (autoboxing) boolean --> Boolean (autoboxing) char --> Character (autoboxing) ArrayList<int> WRONG ArrayList<Integer> RIGHT [COMMON PITFALLS] 1. Forgot import java.util.ArrayList; 2. Using list[0] instead of list.get(0) 3. Removing in for-each loop (ConcurrentModificationException) 4. remove(1) removes INDEX, not value 1 5. HashMap.get() returns null for missing keys 6. HashMap keys must be unique (put replaces) [REMEMBER] 1. import java.util.* for collections 2. ArrayList = ordered, indexed, duplicates OK 3. HashMap = key-value, keys unique, unordered 4. Use generics: ArrayList<String> not ArrayList 5. Use Iterator for safe removal during loop 6. getOrDefault prevents NullPointerException 7. Collections.sort() for sorting, .reverse() for reverse 8. Wrapper classes: Integer not int in generics

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 1: Create and Print ArrayList
practice_exercises.description: Create an ArrayList of 4 colours and print it.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Your first ArrayList! Think of it as a flexible list.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
[Red, Blue, Green, Yellow]
Size: 4

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 2: Access Elements
practice_exercises.description: Get first, last, and middle elements from ArrayList.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: get(0) for first, get(size()-1) for last!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
First: Mon
Last: Fri
Middle: Wed

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 3: Predict -- Add and Remove
practice_exercises.description: Trace through add/remove operations.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Track the list after each operation carefully.
practice_exercises.expected_output: [Z, X, B]

hints.message: After adds: [A,B,C]. After insert X at 1: [A,X,B,C].

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 4: Fix -- Array Syntax on ArrayList
practice_exercises.description: Replace array syntax with ArrayList methods.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: ArrayList uses .get() and .add(), NOT [] brackets.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Alice
Bob

hints.message: ArrayList does not use []. Use .add() to insert.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 5: Build and Print a Number List
practice_exercises.description: Create ArrayList<Integer> with 1-10, print sum.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Use Integer not int for ArrayList generics.

hints.message: ArrayList<Integer> nums = new ArrayList<>(); for(int i=1;i<=10;i++) nums.add(i);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 6: Remove All Occurrences
practice_exercises.description: Remove ALL instances of a value from ArrayList.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: list.remove(obj) only removes the FIRST match. Use a loop!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Before: [A, B, A, C, A]
After: [B, C]

hints.message: Use Iterator to safely remove during loop.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 7: ArrayList contains and indexOf
practice_exercises.description: Search for items in a shopping list.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: contains returns true/false. indexOf returns position or -1.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Has Eggs: true
Has Fish: false
Bread at: 1
Fish at: -1

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 8: Swap Two Elements
practice_exercises.description: Swap elements at two positions in an ArrayList.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Use a temp variable and set() to swap.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Before: [A, B, C, D]
After: [D, B, C, A]

hints.message: Save one value first: String temp = list.get(0);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 9: Copy and Reverse
practice_exercises.description: Create a reversed copy of an ArrayList (without changing original).
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Loop backwards to create reversed copy.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Original: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
Reversed: [5, 4, 3, 2, 1]

hints.message: ArrayList<Integer> reversed = new ArrayList<>(); loop backwards from size()-1 to 0.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 10: Merge Two ArrayLists
practice_exercises.description: Combine two sorted lists into one sorted list.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Add all from both, then sort. Or merge manually.

hints.message: merged.addAll(b) adds all of b to merged. Collections.sort() sorts the combined list.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 11: For-Each Loop
practice_exercises.description: Print each element with for-each loop.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: for(Type var : list) is the simplest loop.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Pet: Dog
Pet: Cat
Pet: Fish
Pet: Bird

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 12: Indexed For Loop
practice_exercises.description: Print with index numbers using traditional for.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Use i < list.size() and list.get(i).
practice_exercises.expected_output:
0: Sword
1: Shield
2: Potion

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 13: Filter into New List
practice_exercises.description: Copy elements matching a condition into a new list.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Loop + if + add to new list = filter pattern.

hints.message: ArrayList<Integer> big = new ArrayList<>(); then loop and check.
ArrayList<Integer> big=new ArrayList<>(); for(int n:nums) if(n>10) big.add(n);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 14: Count Matching Elements
practice_exercises.description: Count how many strings start with a letter.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Loop + condition + counter.
practice_exercises.expected_output: Names starting with A: 4

hints.message: int count=0; for(String n:names) if(n.startsWith("A")) count++;
startsWith() is case-sensitive. 'A' matches Alice, Anna, Alex, Amy.

int count=0; for(String n:names) if(n.startsWith("A")) count++;
System.out.println("Names starting with A: "+count);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 15: Find Maximum Value
practice_exercises.description: Find the largest number in an ArrayList.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Start with first element, compare each.
practice_exercises.expected_output: Max score: 95

hints.message: int max = scores.get(0); then loop comparing.

int max=scores.get(0); for(int s:scores) if(s>max) max=s;
System.out.println("Max score: "+max);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 16: Transform -- Uppercase All
practice_exercises.description: Create new list with all strings uppercased.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Loop + transform + add to new list.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Original: [hello, world, java]
Upper: [HELLO, WORLD, JAVA]

hints.message: ArrayList<String> upper = new ArrayList<>(); for(String w:words) upper.add(w.toUpperCase());
.toUpperCase() returns a new string, original is unchanged.
ArrayList<String> upper=new ArrayList<>(); for(String w:words) upper.add(w.toUpperCase());

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 17: Remove Duplicates
practice_exercises.description: Remove duplicate values from an ArrayList.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Check contains before adding to new list.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Original: [A, B, A, C, B, D]
Unique: [A, B, C, D]

hints.message: !unique.contains(s) checks if s is NOT already in unique. Only adds new ones.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 18: Parallel ArrayLists
practice_exercises.description: Two lists with matching indexes: names and scores.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Same index in both lists = related data.
practice_exercises.expected_output:

Bob: 78
Charlie: 85
Highest: Alice (92)

practice_exercises.ai_intro: Use a second loop or HashMap to count.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Red: 3
Blue: 4
Green: 1

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 20: Iterator Safe Removal
practice_exercises.description: Remove all negative numbers using Iterator.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: for-each + remove = crash. Iterator = safe.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 21: Sort Strings
practice_exercises.description: Sort a list of names alphabetically.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Collections.sort() handles strings automatically.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Before: [Charlie, Alice, Bob, Diana]
Sorted: [Alice, Bob, Charlie, Diana]

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 22: Sort Numbers Descending
practice_exercises.description: Sort numbers from largest to smallest.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Sort ascending then reverse!

hints.message: Collections.sort(nums, Collections.reverseOrder()); // or sort then reverse

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 23: Sort by String Length
practice_exercises.description: Sort words from shortest to longest.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Use Comparator.comparingInt(String::length).
practice_exercises.expected_output: [Cat, Dog, Mouse, Elephant, Hippopotamus]

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 24: Sort Objects by Field
practice_exercises.description: Sort Product list by price.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Comparator.comparingDouble extracts a number field.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 25: Custom Comparator with Lambda
practice_exercises.description: Sort students by grade descending, then name ascending.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Chain comparators for multi-field sorting.
practice_exercises.expected_output: [Alice(92), Diana(92), Bob(85), Charlie(85)]

hints.message: Comparator.comparingInt((Student s) -> s.grade).reversed() for grade descending.
.thenComparing(s -> s.name) chains name sort for ties.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 26: Predict -- Sort Stability
practice_exercises.description: What order after sorting by grade?
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Java sort is stable: equal elements keep original order.
practice_exercises.expected_output: [Alice(80), Charlie(80), Bob(90), Diana(90)]

hints.message: Within the 80s: Alice was first, Charlie second. Stable sort preserves this.
[Alice(80), Charlie(80), Bob(90), Diana(90)] -- stable sort keeps original order for equals.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 27: Binary Search
practice_exercises.description: Use Collections.binarySearch on sorted list.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: List MUST be sorted first! binarySearch is very fast.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Sorted: [Apple, Banana, Cherry, Date]
Cherry at: 2
Fig at: -4

hints.message: Negative result means not found. The value indicates insertion point.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 28: Min, Max, Frequency
practice_exercises.description: Use Collections utility methods.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Collections has min(), max(), frequency() built-in.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Min: 1
Max: 8
Count of 2: 2
Count of 5: 2

practice_exercises.description: Shuffle a list and pick the first element.

Drawn: [varies]

hints.message: String drawn = cards.get(0); or cards.remove(0) to actually draw.

practice_exercises.description: Get the top 3 scores from a list.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Sort descending, then take first 3.

practice_exercises.ai_intro: put(key,value) adds. get(key) retrieves.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
{UK=London, France=Paris, Japan=Tokyo}
France: Paris

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 32: Update and Delete
practice_exercises.description: Modify and remove HashMap entries.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: put with existing key = update. remove deletes.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Before: {Apples=50, Bananas=30, Oranges=25}
After: {Apples=45, Bananas=30, Grapes=60}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 33: Check Key and Value
practice_exercises.description: Use containsKey and containsValue.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: containsKey checks keys, containsValue checks values.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Has admin: true
Has hacker: false
Has value secret123: true
Has value wrongpass: false

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 34: Loop HashMap
practice_exercises.description: Print all entries using entrySet, keySet, values.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: entrySet gives both key and value together.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Alice -> 25
Bob -> 30
Charlie -> 22
Keys: [Alice, Bob, Charlie]
Values: [25, 30, 22]

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 35: getOrDefault
practice_exercises.description: Safely get values with a default for missing keys.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: getOrDefault prevents null when key is missing.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Alice: 95
Eve: 0
Missing get: null

hints.message: scores.getOrDefault("Eve", 0) returns 0 because Eve is missing.
scores.get("Missing") returns null -- can cause NullPointerException!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 36: Word Counter
practice_exercises.description: Count word frequency in a sentence using HashMap.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The getOrDefault pattern is key: map.put(w, map.getOrDefault(w,0)+1).
practice_exercises.expected_output:
java: 3
is: 3
great: 1
and: 2
fun: 1
powerful: 1

hints.message: HashMap<String,Integer> freq=new HashMap<>(); for(String w:words) freq.put(w,freq.getOrDefault(w,0)+1);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 37: Invert a HashMap
practice_exercises.description: Swap keys and values in a HashMap.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Create new map, loop old one, put value as key and key as value.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Original: {UK=London, France=Paris, Japan=Tokyo}
Inverted: {London=UK, Paris=France, Tokyo=Japan}

hints.message:
for(var e:original.entrySet()) inverted.put(e.getValue(),e.getKey());
Note: only works if values are unique!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 38: Predict -- HashMap Overwrite
practice_exercises.description: What happens when you put with an existing key?
practice_exercises.ai_intro: put with same key REPLACES the value.

hints.message: Only the LAST value for "color" survives: green. Size is 2 (two keys).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 39: HashMap + ArrayList Combo
practice_exercises.description: Map each teacher to their list of students.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: HashMap<String, ArrayList<String>> -- each value is a list!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Mr Smith: [Alice, Bob] (2 students)
Ms Jones: [Charlie, Diana, Eve] (3 students)

hints.message: classes.put("Mr Smith", new ArrayList<>()); classes.get("Mr Smith").add("Alice");

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 40: Find Key by Value
practice_exercises.description: Search HashMap for a key that has a specific value.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Loop entrySet and check values.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Score 82: Bob
Score 99: not found

hints.message: Loop entrySet. If entry.getValue() == target, print entry.getKey().
String found=null; for(var e:scores.entrySet()) if(e.getValue()==82) found=e.getKey();
Method: loop all entries, compare value, return key. Return null if not found.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 41: Student List

Charlie (age 19)

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 42: Filter by Field
practice_exercises.description: Get all products under a price threshold.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Loop + condition + add to new list.
practice_exercises.expected_output: Under $100: [Mouse($30.0), Cable($10.0)]

hints.message: ArrayList<Product> result = new ArrayList<>(); for(Product p:list) if(p.price < max) result.add(p);
static ArrayList<Product> filterByPrice(ArrayList<Product> list,double max){ArrayList<Product> r=new ArrayList<>();for(Product p:list)if(p.price<max)r.add(p);return r;}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 43: Find Object by Field
practice_exercises.description: Search for a student by name.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Loop until you find a match, return the object.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Found: Bob(85)
Found: null

hints.message: After the loop, return null (not found).
static Student findByName(ArrayList<Student> list,String name){for(Student s:list)if(s.name.equals(name))return s;return null;}

practice_exercises.description: Average, min, max of object fields.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Loop to accumulate sum, track min/max.
practice_exercises.expected_output:

Min: Charlie (120)
Max: Bob (200)

hints.message: int sum=0; for(Score s:scores) sum+=s.points; double avg=(double)sum/scores.size();
Track minS and maxS: if(s.points<minS.points) minS=s;

B: [Bob(85)]
C: [Charlie(71)]

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 48: Shopping Cart
practice_exercises.description: Cart with add, remove, total methods.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: ArrayList as the backbone of a cart system.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
=== CART ===
Laptop x1 = $999.99
Mouse x2 = $59.98
USB Cable x3 = $29.97
Total: $1089.94

hints.message: static void addItem(String n,double p,int q) { cart.add(new Item(n,p,q)); }
static double total() { double t=0; for(Item i:cart) t+=i.subtotal(); return t; }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 49: Leaderboard
practice_exercises.description: Maintain a sorted top-5 leaderboard.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Add score, sort, keep only top 5.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
=== LEADERBOARD ===
1. Charlie: 700
2. Eve: 600
3. Alice: 500
4. Diana: 450
5. Frank: 350

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 50: Object Equality in Collections
practice_exercises.description: Override equals for correct contains behaviour.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Without equals override, contains checks memory address, not values.
practice_exercises.expected_output: Contains (1,2): true

hints.message: @Override public boolean equals(Object o) { if(!(o instanceof Coordinate)) return false; Coordinate c=(Coordinate)o; return x==c.x && y==c.y; }
Without equals override, contains returns false because it compares memory addresses, not x,y values!

2. [ ] Practice HashMap
3. [ ] Build project

After completing first:

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 52: Inventory System
practice_exercises.description: Track item quantities with HashMap, sell and restock.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: HashMap maps item name to quantity.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Restocked Laptop +10
Restocked Mouse +50
Restocked Cable +100
=== INVENTORY ===
Laptop: 10
Mouse: 50
Cable: 100
Sell 3 Laptops: true
Sell 20 Laptops: false
=== INVENTORY ===
Laptop: 7
Mouse: 50
Cable: 100

hints.message: static boolean sell(String item,int qty){int current=inv.getOrDefault(item,0);if(current<qty)return false;inv.put(item,current-qty);return true;}
getOrDefault handles items not yet in inventory. Check before selling to avoid negative stock.

2. Imagine - Lennon
3. Hey Jude - Beatles
Now playing: Bohemian - Queen
Skipped!
Now playing: Imagine - Lennon
=== PLAYLIST (2 songs) ===
1. Imagine - Lennon
2. Hey Jude - Beatles

hints.message: static void addSong(String t,String a){playlist.add(new Song(t,a));}

static void skip(){if(!playlist.isEmpty()){playlist.remove(0);System.out.println("Skipped!");}}
static void showPlaylist(){System.out.println("=== PLAYLIST ("+playlist.size()+" songs) ===");for(int i=0;i<playlist.size();i++)System.out.println((i+1)+". "+playlist.get(i));}

Misspelled: jumpz (position 5)
Misspelled: lazzy (position 8)

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 56: Student Registry with HashMap
practice_exercises.description: Register students by ID, look up, list all.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: HashMap<String, Student> for ID-based lookup.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
=== REGISTRY (3 students) ===
S001: Alice (age 20)
S002: Bob (age 22)
S003: Charlie (age 19)
Lookup S002: S002: Bob (age 22)
Lookup S999: Not found

hints.message: static void register(String id,String n,int a){registry.put(id,new Student(id,n,a));}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 57: Caesar Cipher with HashMap
practice_exercises.description: Encrypt text using character mapping.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: HashMap maps each letter to its shifted version.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
khoor zruog
mdyd lv ixq

hints.message: static HashMap<Character,Character> buildCipher(int s){HashMap<Character,Character> m=new HashMap<>();for(char c='a';c<='z';c++)m.put(c,(char)('a'+(c-'a'+s)%26));m.put(' ',' ');return m;}
static String encrypt(String t,HashMap<Character,Character> c){StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();for(char ch:t.toCharArray())sb.append(c.getOrDefault(ch,ch));return sb.toString();}
buildCipher maps: a->d, b->e, ..., z->c (shift 3). encrypt replaces each character.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 58: Movie Database
practice_exercises.description: Full CRUD app with ArrayList + HashMap index.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: ArrayList stores data, HashMap provides fast lookup by title.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
All: [Inception (2010) [Sci-Fi] 8.8/10, The Matrix (1999) [Sci-Fi] 8.7/10, Titanic (1997) [Drama] 7.9/10, Interstellar (2014) [Sci-Fi] 8.6/10]
Find: The Matrix (1999) [Sci-Fi] 8.7/10
Sci-Fi: [Inception (2010) [Sci-Fi] 8.8/10, The Matrix (1999) [Sci-Fi] 8.7/10, Interstellar (2014) [Sci-Fi] 8.6/10]
Top: Inception (2010) [Sci-Fi] 8.8/10

hints.message: static void addMovie(String t,String g,int y,double r){Movie m=new Movie(t,g,y,r);movies.add(m);index.put(t,m);}

static Movie findByTitle(String t){return index.get(t);}
static ArrayList<Movie> filterByGenre(String g){ArrayList<Movie> r=new ArrayList<>();for(Movie m:movies)if(m.genre.equals(g))r.add(m);return r;}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 59: Bank Account Manager
practice_exercises.description: Accounts with deposit, withdraw, transfer, statement.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: HashMap for account lookup, ArrayList for transaction history.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
=== A1: Alice ===
Opened with $1000.0
Withdraw $200.0
Balance: $800.0
=== A2: Bob ===
Opened with $500.0
Deposit $200.0
Balance: $700.0

hints.message: static void openAccount(String id,String n,double b){bank.put(id,new Account(id,n,b));}
static boolean transfer(String f,String t,double a){Account from=bank.get(f),to=bank.get(t);if(!from.withdraw(a))return false;to.deposit(a);return true;}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 60: Full Quiz App
practice_exercises.description: Quiz with questions, scoring, review -- capstone.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Everything together: ArrayList, HashMap, objects, sorting.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
=== QUIZ RESULTS ===
Q1: ArrayList uses which import? -> CORRECT
Q2: HashMap.get() returns what for missing key? -> CORRECT
Q3: Which is safe for removal during loop? -> WRONG (You: for-each, Answer: Iterator)
Score: 2/3 (66.7%)

hints.message: static void showResults(){int score=0;System.out.println("=== QUIZ RESULTS ===");for(int i=0;i<quiz.size();i++){Question q=quiz.get(i);System.out.print("Q"+(i+1)+": "+q.text+" -> ");if(q.isCorrect()){score++;System.out.println("CORRECT");}else{System.out.println("WRONG (You: "+q.options[q.userAnswer]+", Answer: "+q.options[q.correct]+")");}}
